questions.question: A streetlight of height 6 m casts a shadow of 8 m for a pole. Calculate the height of the pole using similar triangles.
questions.answer: Let the height of the pole be h. Using similar triangles: height of streetlight/shadow of streetlight = height of pole/shadow of pole. 6/unknown shadow = h/8. Solve for h = (6 × 8)/shadow = depends on given streetlight shadow (assuming shadow of streetlight = 10 m), h = 4.8 m.

questions.question: Explain an experiment to measure the focal length of a concave mirror using a distant object.
questions.answer: Place the concave mirror facing a distant object. Move a screen in front of the mirror to get a sharp image. Measure the distance between mirror and screen; this is approximately the focal length.

questions.question: A candle is placed 15 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm. Find the image position and nature using mirror formula.
questions.answer: Mirror formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u. Here, f = 10 cm, u = -15 cm (real object). 1/v = 1/f - 1/u = 1/10 - (-1/15) = 1/10 + 1/15 = (3 + 2)/30 = 5/30. v = 6 cm. Image is real, inverted, and smaller.

questions.question: Explain how to use a pinhole camera to measure the height of a nearby building.
questions.answer: Measure the distance of the pinhole screen and shadow formed. Use the ratio of image height on screen to actual building height, using similar triangles to calculate the building's height.

questions.question: Describe an experiment to prove the law of reflection using a plane mirror and a protractor.
questions.answer: Place a plane mirror on a table, draw a normal at the center. Shine a ray of light at an angle of incidence i, measure the reflected angle r using a protractor. Verify i = r. Repeat for multiple angles.

questions.question: A convex mirror has a focal length of 12 cm. An object is placed 8 cm from the mirror. Find the image position using the mirror formula.
questions.answer: Mirror formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u. For convex mirror, f = +12 cm, u = -8 cm. 1/v = 1/f - 1/u = 1/12 - (-1/8) = 1/12 + 1/8 = (2 + 3)/24 = 5/24. v = 4.8 cm. Image is virtual, upright, and smaller.

questions.question: Explain why the shadow of an object during sunrise is longer than at noon.
questions.answer: During sunrise, the sun is at a low angle, making light rays strike the object at a shallow angle, creating a long shadow. At noon, sun is overhead, shadows are short.

questions.question: A small opaque object casts a shadow 20 cm long when placed 5 cm from a light source. Calculate the size of the light source using similar triangles if the shadow’s penumbra width is 4 cm.
questions.answer: Using similar triangles, penumbra width/size of object = distance from light source/distance to screen. Solve for light source size: (4/x) = 5/20 → x = 16 cm. Size of light source is 16 cm.

questions.question: Explain the formation of multiple shadows using two light sources and one object.
questions.answer: Each light source forms its own shadow. Where shadows overlap, darker regions (umbra) appear; where only one shadow occurs, it is lighter (penumbra). Experimentally, place two torches at different angles, observe multiple shadows.

questions.question: Using a convex mirror, explain why vehicles have a wider field of view than a plane mirror.
questions.answer: Convex mirrors diverge light rays, producing virtual, smaller, upright images. Divergence allows the driver to see a larger area compared to plane mirrors, which reflect only rays coming directly back.

questions.question: An object is placed between the focal point and a concave mirror. Describe the nature and size of the image.
questions.answer: The image is virtual, upright, and larger than the object because the reflected rays appear to diverge from a point behind the mirror.

questions.question: Explain how a periscope works and why the mirrors are placed at 45° angles.
questions.answer: Light from the object strikes the top mirror at 45°, reflects downward to the bottom mirror at 45°, then to the observer’s eye. The 45° angles ensure correct reflection following the law of reflection.

questions.question: Design an experiment to demonstrate that light travels in straight lines using a pinhole and screen.
questions.answer: Make a cardboard with a pinhole and a screen behind it. Shine light on the pinhole; the image appears on the screen. Move the pinhole slightly; the light passes only through the hole, showing straight-line propagation.

questions.question: Describe the effect of object distance on image size in concave mirrors with diagrams.
questions.answer: When the object is far (>2f), the image is real, inverted, smaller. At 2f, image is real, inverted, same size. Between f and 2f, image is real, inverted, larger. Inside f, image is virtual, upright, enlarged.

questions.question: A screen is placed 30 cm from a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm. Where should the object be placed to get a sharp image on the screen?
questions.answer: Mirror formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u, f = 10, v = 30, 1/10 = 1/30 + 1/u → 1/u = 1/10 - 1/30 = 2/30 = 1/15, u = 15 cm. Object should be 15 cm from mirror.

questions.question: Explain the difference between umbra and penumbra in terms of multiple light sources.
questions.answer: Umbra: fully dark central shadow blocked from all light sources. Penumbra: partially lit outer shadow where some light reaches due to other light sources, creating a gradient.

questions.question: Explain why concave mirrors are used in headlights of vehicles.
questions.answer: Concave mirrors converge light from the bulb into a parallel beam, providing strong, focused illumination on the road ahead.

questions.question: A concave mirror forms a real image three times larger than the object. Find the object distance if the focal length is 12 cm.
questions.answer: Magnification m = -v/u = 3 → v = -3u. Mirror formula 1/f = 1/v + 1/u → 1/12 = 1/(-3u) + 1/u = -1/3u + 1/u = 2/3u → u = 8 cm. Object is 8 cm from mirror.

questions.question: Explain the difference between real and virtual images using ray diagrams.
questions.answer: Real images: formed where rays actually meet, can be projected, inverted (concave mirror beyond f). Virtual images: formed where rays appear to meet, cannot be projected, upright (plane mirror, concave mirror within f).

questions.question: Describe how to construct ray diagrams for image formation by concave mirrors.
questions.answer: Draw principal axis, focal point, object. Draw ray parallel to axis → reflects through focus; ray through focus → reflects parallel; ray through center of curvature → reflects back. Intersection gives image position and nature.

questions.question: How does the position of the light source affect the sharpness of shadows?
questions.answer: Closer light sources produce larger, fuzzier shadows with wider penumbra. Farther, smaller sources produce sharper shadows with narrow penumbra.

questions.question: Explain the difference between convex and concave mirrors in terms of image formation for objects placed at different distances.
questions.answer: Concave mirrors form real/inverted or virtual/upright images depending on object distance. Convex mirrors always form virtual, upright, smaller images, regardless of object distance.

questions.question: Design an experiment to measure the focal length of a convex lens using a distant object.
questions.answer: Place the lens facing a distant object, project the image on a screen. Move screen until the image is sharp. Distance between lens and screen ≈ focal length.

questions.question: Explain how shadows can be used to determine the height of a building using a measuring stick and its shadow.
questions.answer: Measure stick height h_s and its shadow length s_s. Measure building shadow s_b. Using similar triangles: building height h_b = (h_s × s_b)/s_s.

questions.question: A concave mirror has a focal length of 15 cm. An object is placed 20 cm in front of it. Find the image position and magnification.
questions.answer: Mirror formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u, f = 15, u = -20 → 1/v = 1/15 - (-1/20) = 1/15 + 1/20 = (4+3)/60 = 7/60 → v ≈ 8.57 cm. Magnification m = -v/u ≈ -8.57/-20 ≈ 0.43. Image is real, inverted, smaller.
